title: 3GL Development Issues
text: 3GL Development Issues Issues that can affect your Uniface and 3GL development include licensing, multi-threading, signal handling and C++ name mangling. Licensing You need a specific Uniface license code to activate 3GL components. Which one depends on whether you are executing the component on a client or on a server. You need: UCLT3GL on first usage of a 3GL component on a client (pseudo-components are free) USRV3GL on first usage of a 3GL component on a server Multi-Threading Not all Uniface code is guaranteed to be threadsafe. We therefore do not support a multi-threaded call-in application. When you write 3GL code that will be called from Uniface, we recommend that you make that code reentrant and threadsafe. In particular, when you need to use global variables such as errno , stdin , or stdout , which are also used by Uniface code, you must compile your code with the compiler flag that specifies multithreading and/or reentrancy. This will provide the code with its own copy of these variables, so no conflict can arise. Signal Handling If you create a call-in application on Unix, you might want to define a signal handler for Ctrl+C in your main code. Unfortunately, when you call uecreate , Uniface will establish a default signal handler, overwriting the one that you had defined. So, your signal handler must be created after uecreate . The way to do this is have your ProcScript call out to a 3GL function that sets the handler. C++ Name Mangling The C++ compiler creates external function names that do not match the name of your actual function. It appends a string that represents the function’s return type and list of parameters. For example, the function: int myfunc (char* str, int I, long x); Copy might get an external symbol name like: ?myfunc@@YAHPADHJ@Z Copy This is known as C++ name mangling or name decoration. C++ does this so it can distinguish between overloaded functions with the same name. If you want to call a function like this from Uniface, there is a problem, because the ProcScript language does not allow names like this. So, you cannot say: activate "mysig".?myfunc@@YAHPADHJ@Z(a, b, c) Copy Some solutions follow: When using activate , you can put the mangled name in the signature. That is, in your signature, you can define the operation myfunc (with the correct parameters), and give that operation the literal name ?myfunc@@YAHPADHJ@Z . That way, when you say: activate "mysig".myfunc(a, b, c) Copy Uniface will load and call the function ?myfunc@@YAHPADHJ@Z . So if you need to call-out to C++ code, whether home-grown or legacy 3GL, you must inspect the shared library to find out what the function name is (the format of mangled names can vary across different C++ compilers). If you enumerate your entry points in the [USER_3GL] section, you must also use the mangled name, for example: [USER_3GL] my3gl(?myfunc@@YAHPADHJ@Z) Copy Lastly, if you write the C++ code yourself, and do not need to use overloaded functions, you can avoid the issue altogether by telling the compiler not to mangle your function name. This is done by putting the function inside an extern "C" block, like this: extern "C" { EXPORT int myfunc (char* str, int I, long x) { // ... code here ... } } Copy 3GL Component Debugging 3gl debuggers interfere with some operations Uniface normally performs. To avoid such interference, you can set $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING to TRUE . This will allow you to attach a 3GL debugger to an already running instance of Uniface to debug your own 3GL components. This applies both to 3GL components that run using the activate statement and those run using the perform statement. For more details, see $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING . Was this topic helpful?
